title: What 'Ideal' Training Looks Like
concept: In the theoretical ideal, the generator's fakes become so convincing that the discriminator can't do better than a 50/50 coin flip — that's the signal of success.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does it mean for a generator and discriminator to reach a balance point?

options.text: Neither side can improve further by changing its own move alone
The generator has completely stopped working
The discriminator has deleted all its training data

exercises.explain: Right! Now let's find out exactly what that balance point looks like when GAN training goes perfectly.

cards.title: Which twin is which?
cards.body: Imagine identical twins who dress alike, style their hair the same way, and even copy each other's mannerisms. If they get good enough at it, even their own close friends start guessing wrong about half the time. That's the feeling behind an 'ideal' GAN.

cards.title: The ideal end-state: 50/50
cards.body: In the theoretical ideal case, the discriminator ends up unable to do better than random guessing — right about the fake data roughly 50% of the time. That happens because the generator's fakes have become indistinguishable from real data. This 50/50 point is the theoretical 'success' signal for GAN training.

cards.title: The stunt double swap
cards.body: On a movie set, a stunt double might look so much like the lead actor that even the director can't tell them apart on camera — guessing right only about half the time instead of spotting the swap every time. The same idea applies when a generator's fakes become impossible to tell from real data.

cards.title: Watching the accuracy slide toward 50%
cards.body: Round 1: the discriminator is right 95% of the time. Round 50: it's down to 70%. Round 200: it hovers around 50% — a coin flip. That steady slide toward 50% is exactly what 'ideal' training looks like.

cards.title: A worse discriminator isn't a broken one
cards.body: You might think a discriminator sliding down to 50% accuracy means something went wrong or that it stopped learning. In the ideal case, it's the opposite: a discriminator stuck at 50% means the generator's fakes became so convincing that even a fully capable judge can't tell them apart from real data.

cards.title: Weak generator vs. ideal generator
cards.body: Comparing the discriminator's accuracy in each case:

left.text:
Discriminator stays confident
High accuracy (e.g. 90%+)
Fakes are easy to spot

right.text:
Discriminator can't do better
Accuracy near 50%
Fakes are indistinguishable

exercises.prompt: In the theoretical ideal case, what happens to the discriminator's accuracy?

options.text: It drops to about 50%, no better than random guessing
It rises to 100%, correctly spotting every fake
It stays exactly wherever it started
It becomes irrelevant and stops being measured

exercises.explain: The ideal end-state is the discriminator reduced to a coin flip, because the fakes are indistinguishable from real data.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A discriminator accuracy near 50% is a sign that GAN training has failed.
exercises.explain: False — in the theoretical ideal case, 50% accuracy is actually the success signal: it means the fakes became indistinguishable from real data.

exercises.sentence: In the ideal case, the discriminator ends up doing no better than ___ because the generator's fakes are indistinguishable from real data.
exercises.explain: Random guessing — about 50% — is the theoretical signal that a generator's fakes have become truly convincing.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario best matches the 'ideal' GAN training outcome?

options.text: A discriminator that used to be 95% accurate is now only right about 50% of the time on the generator's latest fakes
A discriminator that stays 99% accurate no matter how much the generator trains
A generator that stops producing any output at all

exercises.explain: A discriminator sliding down to coin-flip accuracy (50%) is the textbook sign of an ideal, fully-converged generator.

exercises.prompt: A team reports their discriminator can only guess real vs. fake correctly about half the time on their generator's newest images — no better than flipping a coin. How should they interpret this, in the theoretical ideal sense?

options.text: This is the theoretical 'success' signal — the generator's fakes have become indistinguishable from real data
The discriminator is malfunctioning and must be rebuilt
The generator has stopped producing any images
This means the training data was deleted

exercises.explain: Exactly — 50/50 is the ideal finish line for a GAN. But here's the twist: real-world training almost never actually gets there cleanly. Let's see why next.
